speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is June 7, 1995. Survivor Norbert Friedman, interviewer Mark Goldberg. We're in West Hempstead, New York in the USA. The language is English. You were going to say something about when the Ukrainian guards came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in 1943, when the SS came in and it became a concentration camp, before we were tattooed-- the Ukrainian guards came into the camp to build a barrack for the [GERMAN] and also a bond for his horses. And they bragged about this man and what a hero he was, that he single-handed-- supposedly-- he executed the town of Przemysl. He killed single-handed about 600 people. And then he gave his handgun to his six-year-old son to kill children. So when this man came in, we were rather concerned about his behavior. And whatever reputation preceded him was justified. He had a horse-- he had a dog by the name of Prince, who was trained to jump at your throat if you didn't take your hat off, or go for your testicles if you passed by with your hands in your pockets. He also had two perfectly matching white horses. And here's this man, who during the week, would come into the camp, kill people or sentence them to death, or beat them. And then on Sunday, he would hitch the two horses, take his wife and take his son on a very leisurely Sunday drive. He led a totally normal life, just outside the camp, which was in [GERMAN] I was in his apartment. I was awakened one morning when I was on the night shift, to come in and fix the toy that his boy had. He had a tank that worked on a spring that he wound up. And the spring apparently broke. And I looked at it, and I said I have to take the tank into the shop to fix it. And I was bending down, taking it apart, he kicked me in the behind. And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He says, hurry up, otherwise I'll kill you, you dirty Jew. And this was the child that the Ukrainian guards were telling us that his father gave him a handgun to shoot children. In 1944-- Did

speaker: interviewee
text: Whether it was story-- I read the book, and I tried to find any documentation on that. There's nothing mentioned on that. Whether it was only stories that the Ukrainians were telling it or it was a fact, I have no way to substantiate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular man, as I told you, had two matching horses. And one night, a friend of mine had a tryst with a-- just shortly before, they brought 100 women to our camp from another labor camp. So he had a love affair with one of the girls. He went into the barn. And one of the horses walked out and walked on the electrified wire-- and by that time, the wires became electrified when the SS took over-- and was killed. And [GERMAN] told us that unless we produce an identical horse, he's going to shoot 20 people. And again, we had to go to our contacts on the outside with some Polish people. And they found in the village someplace-- anyhow, after two days, they brought a horse that was a matching horse to this. It was a gray-white horse. And it cost of course sums of money, to pay off the Poles, in order to get this horse. This particular Schwammberger was in charge of our transport in July 1944, when the camp was being liquidated. It was the advance of the Russian army. They started liquidating the camps and moved west. So on July 24, our camp was moved to Wieliczka, which was a town outside Krakow. And there was another camp there. When we heard about the advancing-- through the contacts with the underground-- about the advancing Russian army, a group of us young people, we had a tunnel dug from the camp to the outside. And the last minute decision we made was not to use it. Because the most that could escape was 20, 25 people. And we felt that we're going to jeopardize hundreds of people. And we didn't know whether we're going to survive, but we surely will sign a death edict for many people. And we did not use the escape route. And we were loaded on trains, and we were taken to Wieliczka. And Wieliczka was a salt mine. They had a salt mine. The camp was around the salt mine. There was one part of the camp was for the women, and one part was for the men. Several weeks later, all the people from our camp were rounded up and put on the transport. We didn't know where we were going, but we assumed that we're either going to one of the extermination camps or another labor camp. And under conditions such as we lived, you develop a very keen sense of being able to determine whom you can trust, who are the people that, when push comes to pull, will not betray you. And we tried to surround ourselves with those people, and go into the trains with that group of people. But that was impossible. So you had maybe 20 or 25 people in the particular car that were friendly with you, and the rest were other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason I'm trying to tell you the story, after the war, I was approached by a young friend of mine, a childhood survivor, he was a professor of psychology at UCLA, who has written many books on the Holocaust and on the second generation. And he approached me, he said to me, how could you retain your faith in God under conditions such as you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Contrary to the majority of the people who gave up their faith, who became skeptic and who copped out, it was very difficult for me to find the reasons for it. And after a long time of deliberation, I came to the conclusion that the examples of-- it was not the norm. It was the exceptions that made me think of who I am and of God and the piety and the allegiance to Jewish faith. One of them was what I told you about-- the request for execution. The other one was an incident that I want to briefly describe. We had a man called Reb Meyer, who came from a small town. I don't remember the name of the village anymore. He was a very, very observant man. Small in stature-- very small man. But very noticeable because he always would go around and perk up the spirit. He was working on the outside in the garden detail. And at the risk of his own safety, would bring food and food to the camp for the sick. He would administer confession, the [NON-ENGLISH], to the people who were dying, stay with them. And you could find his lonely silhouette in the evening, praying some place at the end of the barracks. He was truly a person who believed in the laws that governed the Jewish people during the holidays or Passover. He wouldn't eat, he wouldn't eat the bread that was distributed. He would hoard potatoes and live on the potatoes. And he was one of the men that was put in our car. You have to understand they put about 110 people into one cattle car. And our transport set out, and after a few hours, we got to Auschwitz. And we stayed at the sidings. And all we know that the [GERMAN] was negotiating with the officials at Auschwitz not to unload us. Because we were defense workers, and our destination was to go to Germany, as we later found out. While we were there, we could see the chimneys smoking. And how callous were we at that time that we were even remarking that they must be burning children. Because the smoke is lighter from the chimneys. While we were standing at the siding, they brought a transport from Holland. They came in Pullman cars, dressed. They thought they were going to some labor camp someplace. And with suitcases they were being unloaded. And we could see the SS and the Jewish camp inmates taking them and separating them. Fortunately, after the four hours, the [GERMAN] won over, and we proceeded. The reason that he fought so hard for us is because if he lost us, he would have gone to the Russian front. This way, he had those Jews to watch. And we went on. And this is in August, 1944. It was terribly hot. We were kept in the cattle cars. There was no room to sit down, so we managed to design a way by which we would spread our legs, and the people would sit one next to the other. There was no air, so we designed a rotation system where each and every one from the part of the car would stand at the little window for a while in the four corners. On the third day, it was so unbearable that we were drinking-- we were licking the salty condensation on the walls. There was no toilet facilities, so we dug out with our hands a hole in the bottom of the train. Two people already took cyanide, committed suicide. And we propped them up against the wall. The nerves were so frail and so much on edge that fights were breaking out. When we pulled out to a station in a large city, Nuremberg, and we stopped at the station. And there were some army nurses walking around, because there were transports of soldiers going back and forth. And all you could hear the cries of [GERMAN] through the windows, because this is already the third day we had no food, no water. And as fate wanted, Reb Meyer-- the one that I spoke about, that observant person-- was at one of the windows at that particular time. And he yelled out for water. And one of the nurses mercifully brought him a bottle of water. And he took it to the bars. And everybody in the car became very tense. The situation was-- you could cut the air. And had he taken the bottle of water and drank from it, I'm sure that they would fallen upon him and tore it out of his hands. And he took the bottle of water, didn't even touch it with his lips. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He turned to the next to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he says, drink [GERMAN]. So there's enough. Drink, my child, but just a little bit, so there's enough for the next one. And this act of his froze the whole car. And that bottle of water kept passing on. Even after it was empty, it kept passing on. And it was like something-- we became transformed. His act of self-sacrifice [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] returned to us our dignity. And when we finally got to Flossenburg on the fourth day, I think for that time, for that moment, we were different people. Reb Meyer expired in the Camp Flossenburg about a month before liberation. But to many of those who were in this particular car, his act was something that was a beacon of hope and faith. We got to the Camp of Flossenburg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a revelation to us, because it was the first camp in Germany where the Jews were not anymore. We arrived there on Sunday, I think. We walked out from the cars. And it took us a while before our legs were able to function. And they marched us to the camp. And it was on Sunday. And we saw people working outside, tending the gardens and the flowers. There were inmates. And some of them told us that the whole Austrian government is there [GERMAN], and the Austrian government was incarcerated over there. And we were brought into what they called-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call-- when they quarantine barracks. We were stripped naked. And for four days and four nights, we were kept naked. I know very few things that dehumanize you as much as being naked with a large group of people. We would get up in the morning-- the frost was still on the ground-- and we huddle together. We would embrace each other to feed off each other's warmth. On the fourth day, they have shaven our pubic hair and our hair under the arms, slapped us with Lysol, and they've taken us to the showers. We were not sure whether they were showers or they were-- because at that time, we knew about gassing through the showers. But they were showers. And they gave us uniforms. Some of us were picked for internal work in the camp. Most of us were just milling around within, for several weeks. Until one day they came out, and they lined us up. And they inspected us bodily. And they marked us with a chemical pencil, over the head, on the forehead, with letters. Three slash four, one slash three, different markings. We didn't know what it was. And they told us, if we removed the markings, we're going to suffer a punishment for it. And then finally we found out that these markings were for different designations. And the group that was marked the way I was and my father, we went to Camp Leitmeritz in Czechoslovakia. The other group went to Mauthausen, from which very few survived. We arrived in Leitmeritz, which is a Czech name for it was Litomerice, which is about six kilometers outside Theresienstadt. We were the first Jews in that particular camp. And the Polish inmates staged a pogrom. They attacked us, as we moved in our barracks. They took the boards from the bunk beds. And they started beating us, yelling, kill the Jew! Kill the Jew! To us, it was something that we couldn't comprehend. I mean, these were fellow inmates. But because we were Jewish and they were Polish, so they staged a pogrom. And if it wasn't for the SS that came with bayonets on their carbines and the machine guns, probably many people would have gotten killed. That particular camp, the people in the camp worked in quarries. They were mining over there. Later, we found out that there was uranium in those mountains and it was for the heavy water or whatever they needed it. So we worked in the quarries in the underground. In that camp, I came down with my first attack of malaria. And I was put into a dispensary. But I stayed in the dispensary during the night. And during the day, my friends used to help me out and help me to walk to the work detail. Because there was a German officer who was supposedly a medical student and used to come during the day and used to experiment with the people in the sick bay, inject air or liquid and then kill them that way. The conditions were very, very bad. Once a week, we were able to take a 20-second cold shower. The food that we were given could hardly sustain us. We were slowly losing strength. My very close friend Milek Salpeter from Tarnow lost three of his toes when the lorry went over his foot. And he was put in a dispensary. And on the day we were told that we're being moved from Leitmeritz, I said goodbye to him, because I didn't expect to ever see him again. And I gave him my soup ration. Fortunately, he survived. He's in Australia today. And we were moved to Dachau. We travelled to Dachau for three days and three nights in cattle cars, without any food, without any water. We arrived in Dachau. We came to the [GERMAN], which is the square of assembly. We were lined up. And they put out kettles with boiled cabbage and potatoes and peels. And they were just about to open up and start feeding. We could smell the cabbage. There was 2,000 people lined up, hungry, like animals. And the alarm went on. There was an air raid. And we were told to go into ditches. And we all had to disperse into the ditches. And the air raid lasted quite a long time. While we were sitting there, we were praying for the American planes to bomb us and bomb our tormentors. But all they did drop leaflets, to tell us that they knew we are there. They bombed the industrial facilities not far outside Dachau. Some of the inmates could not contain themselves, because they were so hungry. And they ran out during the air raid. They ran out, and opened up the kettles and started grabbing the food. And the SS, from the towers, machine gunned them. So later on when we lined up to eat, we were stepping over the bodies of our dead comrades who could not control their hunger. We stayed in Dachau for several weeks. I think on the second week of our presence over there, we were told that we'll have to go to [GERMAN]. We will be given new numbers. We're going to change our clothes, we're going to be given new numbers, and we're going to go to another camp. While we were in Poland, in the camps, each of us had some personal belongings. We used to hide them and keep them. But once we came to Flossenburg, to Germany, we were stripped of everything that we owned. All we were given is the striped uniform, the wooden clogs, and a metal plate on our [INAUDIBLE], into which to receive our food. And the spoon, the handle of it, we would thin out with a rock or something, to serve as a knife. Because you were not allowed to have a knife. All other private possessions were forbidden. I managed to hide two things. One was a picture of my mother. And the other one was a toothbrush. The picture of my mother, I kept in order to be able to dream about my home, the love, and the warmth of pre-war life. And the toothbrush was my only connection to civilized life. The very mundane function of brushing your teeth-- even without any toothpaste, with bleeding gums, and I lacked quite a few teeth at that time from the beatings-- it was what kept me close to civilization. It was my contact with normality. So these things, I was able to hide on my body. And when we got to Dachau, we had to go to the [GERMAN]. We knew that we're going to be searched. And I took-- I hid in a rag the picture of my mother and my toothbrush, and I hid it under a barrack, when we went to [GERMAN]. And I made sure that nobody saw it. Apparently, somebody must have seen them. When I came out, it was gone. For me, this was the end. At that time, I made the decision to kill myself. Because there was nothing for me to live for-- not memories. And I lost contact from being a human being. And I marched towards the-- I went where the electrified wires were. I was determined to commit suicide. And somebody told my father, who was with me at that time. And he came running. And he took me away and persuaded-- but I went into a very deep depression that lasted for quite a long time after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was with you through each of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father survived through all the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through all the camps. You'll say it's coincidence, it took some doings on occasion. Because-- in Flossenburg when we were being marked, the marks were different. My father stepped out in front of the whole assembly and went over to the SS colonel who was in charge of-- and he addressed him in German. He told him that he was a soldier in the Austro-Hungarian army, and please could he have his relative with him. And that man turned around-- he wouldn't even face my father-- and said to the agent, he says, [GERMAN]. Let the Jew have his son. And we were allowed to be together. My father was a man of extreme courage. He risked his life many times to save others. I happened to have been attending a wedding where eight of the people at my table were survivors from the camp. And six of them told that night-- each of them told the story-- how my father saved his life in the camp, while he worked in the kitchen or how he protected them. Things that I wasn't even aware of. From Dachau, we went to Augsburg, which-- really there's nothing that is very distinguished about Augsburg. We live in the big barracks. The only thing that I remember clearly is while we were in Augsburg, they brought a transport of Hungarians. These were the first Hungarian Jews that we encountered. And we tried very hard to give them all the wisdom, the camp wisdom that we possessed, which they didn't have. We tried to tell them not to get in line the second times, because the kapo would recognize him and administer beating. They used to trade their bread rations for cigarettes. And they used to get sick and die from hunger. They were not conditioned the way we were conditioned. We were conditioned-- 1944, we were already five years of war. And we gradually became-- those of us who were alive, we acquired certain savvy that these people were lacking. The other thing that now I remember, we worked in a factory where we were sorting out nuts and bolts. And that particular factory, it's the only time we were bombed by the Americans. And several of us were killed, and some SS. And we were actually-- we were happy. We lost people, we were rejoicing. That particular day, we were rejoicing. From that camp, we used to go on a detail to another factory in a place called Horgau. We used to go by train there. And my father worked there, and apparently he ruined something. He was not a mechanic. And I remember before we were going back to the train station, there was my father hanging by his arms. And this was one of the hardest things that I had to endure, to stay in line and look at my father hanging and controlling myself not to break out from the line and trying to help him. And my father-- in his eyes-- they made sure that he hung. His feet were like six inches from the ground, and he was suspended in the air. When we came back to the camp, it took a long time before-- they had to put the arms back in place-- and before he was able to work. From Horgau, we went to Leonberg. Leonberg was a camp outside Stuttgart. The Germans took the two tunnels that the autobahn went through. They closed up the two tunnels, and they made a factory, in which they were building fuselages and wings for the first jet planes for the Messerschmitt 262s. We lived in a camp outside the thing. And we worked, particularly in those tunnels. One night, I had an attack of malaria. And when the air raid alarm sounded, everybody had to get up and run to the tunnels. I made the decision not to go, because I wasn't able to get out of bed. I had temperature, and I stayed in bed. And somebody-- when the lights went on after the all-clear sounded, somebody [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] denounced me to one of the SS. And I was brought down, and I received a terrible beating, terrible beating. The man, he went out of his way. By the time my father came back from the camp, I was laying there and blood was coming from my ears, my nose, my mouth. And again, I said to my father, I haven't got the strength. I said, please let me go. I don't want to make it anymore. I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father said, look, there's redemption. He says to me, you know what's tonight? I said, no, I don't. He said, tonight is Hanukkah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And again, he kept my spirits up. Wonderful man. We'll have to end this tape.
